title: Anatomical Pathology Technologist
how_to_become: To become an anatomical pathology technologist in the NHS, apply for training opportunities on the NHS recruitment website.

salary.entry_note: Qualified anatomical pathology technologists start at £31,537 before tax. Trainees are paid £24,012, increasing to £31,537 on completion of the training programme.
salary.experienced_note: Qualified anatomical pathology technologists can earn up to £34,303 before tax.

description: Anatomical pathology technologists work in hospital mortuaries assisting pathologists with post-mortems. The purpose of a post-mortem is to examine a body to determine the cause of death.
role_overview: As an anatomical pathology technologist, you'll make sure mortuary and post-mortem services run smoothly, assisting with post-mortems, arranging viewings of the deceased, keeping records, and overseeing legal documentation.

entry_requirements.overview: No specific prior qualifications are required. A good standard of education is expected.

work_experience: Work placements and volunteering can provide healthcare experience and help when applying to college or a new job with NHSScotland.

education_and_training.training_programme: Level 3 Diploma in Healthcare Science (Anatomical Pathology Technology), equivalent to SCQF level 6. A distance-learning course accredited by the Royal Society for Public Health.
education_and_training.registration: On completion, you can join the Academy for Healthcare Science Practitioner Register.

learning_and_development.overview: Anatomical pathology technologists must keep skills and knowledge up to date through Continuing Professional Development (CPD).

career_progression.overview: Additional qualifications support progression into senior roles with added responsibilities.
qualifications: Level 4 Diploma in Anatomical Pathology Technology — for associate anatomical pathology technologists, covering advanced practices, mortuary procedures, and managerial skills.

chunks.text: To become an anatomical pathology technologist in the NHS, apply for training opportunities on the NHS recruitment website.

Anatomical pathology technologists start at £31,537 before tax. Trainees are paid £24,012, increasing to £31,537 on completion of training. Experienced technologists can earn up to £34,303 before tax.
Anatomical pathology technologists work in hospital mortuaries assisting pathologists with post-mortems to determine the cause of death.

Anatomical pathology technologists ensure mortuary and post-mortem services run smoothly, assisting with post-mortems, arranging viewings of the deceased, keeping records, and overseeing legal documentation.
Useful school subjects include Biology, Chemistry, Physics, English, and Maths. No specific prior qualifications are required.

Training involves the Level 3 Diploma in Healthcare Science (Anatomical Pathology Technology) at SCQF level 6, a distance-learning course accredited by the Royal Society for Public Health. On completion, technologists can join the Academy for Healthcare Science Practitioner Register.
CPD is required throughout the career, with training courses, conferences, and seminars provided by the Association of Anatomical Pathology Technology (AAPT).

The Level 4 Diploma in Anatomical Pathology Technology covers advanced practices, mortuary procedures, and managerial skills, supporting progression to senior roles including mentoring trainees and managing mortuary staff.
Anatomical pathology technologists can join the Academy for Healthcare Science (AHCS), Association of Anatomical Pathology Technology (AAPT), and Royal Society for Public Health.
